//: Per-version upgrade records applied by `update` to already-installed harnesses. Keys are released versions; each entry may carry: removed (stale template paths to delete when sha-unmodified), renamed (old path -> new path), promotedModules (opt-in modules folded into the default harness), configSteps (gate steps injected into the consumer's gate config: {name, cmd, after, array}; array defaults to VALIDATE_STEPS — the 22-gate floor chain — and is 'STOP_HOOK_STEPS' for the non-floor turn-fatal checks. harness.config.mjs is SEEDED, so `update` never overwrites it: this injection is the ONLY way a new step reaches an existing install), configCommandUpdates (from-guarded canonical-command rewrites: {name, from, to}), seedOnInitOnly (NEW seeded exemplar paths a newer template ships as init-time-only starting content — `update` must NOT auto-plant them into an existing install; a trailing '/' matches the whole subtree, no slash matches an exact file; collected across ALL versions, not just pending, and pullable on demand via `update --refresh-seeded <path>`). Entries are applied in version order for manifest.harnessVersion < v <= installer version. See installer/lib/migrations.mjs.

0.1.6.//: packages/schema/drizzle/0002_notes_keyset_idx.sql is seedOnInitOnly for a reason that is NOT cosmetic: drizzle/ is a seeded PREFIX, so `update` would otherwise auto-plant a NEW MIGRATION into an existing project's schema — DDL they never wrote, applied to their database on the next db:migrate, with a filename that may collide with their own 0002_*. The harness does not write migrations for you. A fresh install gets the composite keyset index as part of its exemplar; an upgraded install is told to author its own by the DAL plan probe, which reds on the Sort and names the index shape (see tests/rls/plan-regression.test.ts). || tools/mutation-baseline.json and tools/test-quality-allow.json are seedOnInitOnly for the same class of reason: both record a PROJECT'S OWN JUDGEMENTS ("this surviving mutant is genuinely equivalent, and here is why"; "this test is deliberately pending"). Planting the template's judgements into someone else's repo on `update` would silently ACCEPT survivors they never reviewed — the exact opposite of what the ratchet is for. An upgraded install therefore has no baseline, and the ratchet self-disables with an adoption NOTE until they seed their own: `pnpm mutation:baseline`, write a reason for every survivor, commit. || tools/native-perf-budget.json is seedOnInitOnly for a THIRD reason, structural rather than judgemental: its subjects[] enumerate THIS project's #[tauri::command] surface, and its partner (apps/desktop/src-tauri/benches/host.rs) lives under the seeded `apps/` prefix, which `update` never overwrites. Planting the budget alone would arm a closure check against a bench that is not there; planting the bench alone would inject code calling a configure_app() signature the consumer's own lib.rs does not have, and their crate would stop compiling. So `update` plants NEITHER, the gate self-disables with an adoption NOTE naming the three-step recipe, and `graduate` refuses while that NOTE stands.
